events.title: Blackfoot River sighting
events.photoCaption: Floated the Blackfoot River with Andrew. High water!

events.title: Salmon fly hatch sighting
events.photoCaption: Salmon flies were flitting around all day. Won't be long before the blizzard arrives.
photoNote: Salmon flies (Pteronarcys californica) are among the largest stoneflies in North America — adults can reach nearly 2 inches. They hatch once a year, usually triggered by water temps climbing into the mid-40s °F, and the hatch window on a given stretch of river is only a few days.
When it happens, it's chaos. The bugs come off in clouds so thick you can smell them. Trout gorge themselves to the point of being almost unhookable — they barely need to move to eat.

Waldo spotted this cluster clinging to streamside willows near the takeout at the old Johnsrud bridge. Andrew reckoned the main event was still a day or two out upriver. The water was running high and fast — maybe 6,000 cfs — which pushes the fish tight to the banks and into the slower eddies.
This is why people plan trips to the Blackfoot in June.
